# Why Interpretability? An Anthropology of Machines {#sec-why-interpretability}

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple" icon="false"}
**🚧 Status: `drafted`** — a machine-assisted draft built from my teaching materials, awaiting my full revision. Read it as a working paper, not a finished chapter — and tell me what breaks in the comments below.
:::

Suppose you did everything the earlier parts of this book teach. You wrote a careful prompt, validated it against a hand-coded sample, and had a language model label 40,000 parliamentary speeches for populist rhetoric — @sec-batch made the scale trivial. Agreement with your human coders: high enough to publish. Then a reviewer asks the only question that matters: *what did the model actually measure?* Populism — or anger? Anti-elite framing — or just informal register? You cannot answer by looking at the outputs, because the outputs are exactly what is in question. With a human coder you could ask. With a dictionary method you could read the word list. With this instrument, until very recently, the honest answer was: nobody knows. Not you, and not the engineers who built it.

This part of the book is about the fact that "nobody knows" has stopped being the whole truth. There is now a young science of reading the inside of a language model — the field calls itself *interpretability* — and my argument in this chapter is that it is not, at heart, a computer-science problem. Studying a system you did not design, that comes with no manual, through its behavior and its internal structure — that is what social scientists do for a living. This chapter makes the case in four steps: these models have a biology; a 36-year-old philosophical argument about them has become an empirical question; there are exactly two ways to study a mind, and this part of the book is organized around them; and the skills the young field is missing are the ones you already have. No code in this chapter. The code starts in @sec-logit-lens.

<!-- DRAFT NOTE (Emre): this chapter adapts my SICSS-Istanbul 2026 talk ("Anthropology of Machines"). The talk opened with two live polls — "Are LLMs black boxes?" and "Does AI need social science, or does social science need AI?" — and the room's votes shaped the evening. A sentence or two about what the room actually said would open this chapter well. -->

Before you read on, answer the talk's question for yourself, on the record: **does AI need social science — or does social science need AI?** Hold your answer. We come back to it.

## These models have a biology

Five years ago, if you asked how a language model reached an answer, the honest reply was: nobody knows. Let me show you what changed. Watch the first two minutes of Anthropic's short film [*Tracing the thoughts of a large language model*](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Bj9BD2D3DzA) before you continue — this book has no required videos, but this one earns its few minutes. What you see there is not an artist's illustration. It is the model's own internal activity, made visible: features lighting up, the model choosing a rhyme word *before* it writes the line that has to reach it, the same concept firing across English, French, and Chinese.

Now look at what the researchers called the accompanying paper: [*On the Biology of a Large Language Model*](https://transformer-circuits.pub/2025/attribution-graphs/biology.html) ([Lindsey et al. 2025](https://transformer-circuits.pub/2025/attribution-graphs/biology.html)). Not the *engineering* of a language model. The *biology*. When the people who build a thing reach for that word, they are telling you what kind of science it now takes to understand it.

The word is earned, and here is why. Nobody wrote the inside of these models. Engineers write a training procedure — a recipe — and then billions of numbers arrange themselves under gradient descent, the same process you watched in miniature in @sec-neural-networks. As Anthropic's Chris Olah puts it, these systems are ["grown more than they are built"](https://www.darioamodei.com/post/the-urgency-of-interpretability) — the line is popularized in Dario Amodei's essay [*The Urgency of Interpretability*](https://www.darioamodei.com/post/the-urgency-of-interpretability) (2025), which is the best short statement of why the builders themselves are alarmed by their own ignorance. Inside a grown system there are parts that behave like anatomy — *features* and *circuits*, internal structures that nobody designed and nobody can read off a blueprint. So the people who build these models now study them the way a biologist studies a cell: observe, dissect, intervene.

This is not a metaphor I invented for the chapter title. In 2019, before ChatGPT existed, twenty-three researchers published a manifesto in *Nature* called [*Machine behaviour*](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41586-019-1138-y) ([Rahwan et al. 2019](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41586-019-1138-y)), arguing that intelligent machines should be studied empirically, with the methods of ethology and behavioral science, by people who are not their makers. The call to study machines like organisms came from *Nature*. Anthropic answered it, six years later, with a paper titled *biology*. The remaining question is who else shows up to do the fieldwork.

::: {.callout-warning}
Keep the fine print of the biology paper in view, because I will hold you to the same standard later. The 2025 circuit work was done on Claude 3.5 Haiku — a small production model — on *hand-picked prompts*, and an attribution graph is a plausible story about one prompt, not causal proof of a general mechanism. The researchers say this themselves. "We can now see inside" is true; "we now understand these models" is not. The honest status is: translucent, in patches — and the patches are growing.
:::

<!-- DRAFT NOTE (Emre): in the talk this section was a cold open — 90 seconds of the video played in silence before I said a word. If you have a note on how the room reacted, it belongs here. -->

## An argument that became an experiment

To see why this is *your* problem, I have to take you back to an argument from 1988.

The reigning theory of mind then said: to think is to manipulate symbols. A mind runs rules over discrete tokens — the way [ELIZA](https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/365153.365168), the 1966 chatbot ([Weizenbaum 1966](https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/365153.365168)), matched "I am sad" against a pattern and returned "why are you sad," only grander. [Fodor and Pylyshyn (1988)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/0010027788900315) made the canonical case that real thought works this way, and their strongest argument was *systematicity*: you have never met a person who understands "the dog chased the cat" but cannot understand "the cat chased the dog." Thought comes in recombinable bundles. That, they argued, is only possible if thoughts are built from parts — symbols, with rules for combining them. A neural network that merely tunes continuous weights has no such parts. Therefore a network can *imitate* a concept, but it can never *possess* one. That argument shaped a generation of cognitive science.

The modern reply arrived in 2024, from cognitive scientists led by Steven Piantadosi, under a title whose hedge is doing honest work: [*Why concepts are (probably) vectors*](https://www.cell.com/trends/cognitive-sciences/fulltext/S1364-6613(24)00171-2). You met the argument in @sec-embeddings: a concept is not a symbol but a *direction* in a high-dimensional space. Directions can be combined and recombined — the systematicity Fodor demanded — but with something symbols never had: distance, similarity, degree. A symbol system can say that *taxes* and *budget* are different symbols; it has no native way to say they are close. A vector space says it with a number.

| | symbols (Fodor & Pylyshyn) | vectors (Piantadosi et al.) |
|---|---|---|
| a concept is | a discrete token | a direction in a space |
| combination | rules over tokens | vector operations |
| systematicity | built in | achievable |
| similarity, degree | not native | built in |
| "is it really in there?" | unanswerable from outside | *checkable* — go look |

The last row of that table is the hinge of this whole part of the book. In 1988 the dispute was philosophy: there was no system that plausibly had concepts, so there was nothing to check. Today there is. Language models plainly recombine ideas — and we no longer have to argue from behavior alone. We can open one up and ask the literal question: is the concept in there, sitting as a direction? Fodor and Piantadosi could only debate it. **We can go look.** Thirty-six years of philosophy of mind quietly became an empirical research program, and @sec-sparse-autoencoders is where you run it yourself.

## Two ways to study a mind

There are two ways to study any mind, and the distinction organizes everything that follows, so I want it stated plainly before any method appears.

You can **observe**: read the system's internal state without changing anything. You can **intervene**: change something inside — edit it, ablate it, steer it — and watch what breaks. Observation tells you what is *there*; intervention tells you what it is *for*. The distinction is the same one you know from your own methods training — description versus causal identification — and it carries the same epistemic weights. A pattern you observe is a correlation. Only an intervention licenses a causal claim. Interpretability researchers learned this the hard way: you can train a probe that reads some quantity out of a model's activations with high accuracy and still have no evidence the model *uses* that quantity — a running lesson in [David Bau's interpretability course](https://neural-mechanics.baulab.info), whose slogan compresses it: what an AI knows is not always what it says. Accessible is not the same as used.

I think of the observational tradition as the *ethnography of the model*. You sit with the system, you watch its internal life unfold, you take field notes, you build a description — and you do not touch anything. That is what the next two chapters do, and everything in them is non-invasive reading of the residual stream, the "running notes" channel you met in @sec-transformers:

- The **logit lens** (@sec-logit-lens) reads the model's running guess at every layer — you watch an answer form across depth before the model has said a word. It began as a pseudonymous [blog post](https://www.lesswrong.com/posts/AcKRB8wDpdaN6v6ru/interpreting-gpt-the-logit-lens) ([nostalgebraist 2020](https://www.lesswrong.com/posts/AcKRB8wDpdaN6v6ru/interpreting-gpt-the-logit-lens)) — this field is young enough that a blog post can found a method. Simple as it is, it has produced striking findings: [Wendler et al. (2024)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2402.10588) used it to show that multilingual models prompted in French seem to pass through English-like representations in their middle layers — a private language beneath the spoken one. (Striking, and honestly still contested — it may be an artifact of the vocabulary. A reading, not a proof.)
- **Sparse autoencoders** (@sec-sparse-autoencoders) name the concepts inside. The obvious approach — read the model neuron by neuron — fails, because a single neuron fires for dogs, and Tuesdays, and sarcasm: models pack more concepts than they have neurons, so concepts share wires. That is *superposition* ([Elhage et al. 2022](https://arxiv.org/abs/2209.10652)), and notice what it implies: the neuron is the wrong unit of analysis, and the concept is a direction smeared across many neurons — exactly Piantadosi's claim, discovered independently by people trying to read the wires. Sparse autoencoders unmix the signal into thousands of readable *features* ([Bricken et al. 2023](https://transformer-circuits.pub/2023/monosemantic-features/index.html); scaled to a production model in [Templeton et al. 2024](https://transformer-circuits.pub/2024/scaling-monosemanticity/index.html)).

Then the part crosses the line from watching to touching:

- **Activation patching** (@sec-activation-patching) swaps internal states between two runs of the model to locate which ones actually cause a behavior — the do-operator, applied to a mind. Its ancestor result is worth knowing now: [Meng et al. (2022)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2202.05262) located where a factual association lives and then *edited* it, making a model believe the Eiffel Tower is in Rome.
- **Circuits** (@sec-circuits) assemble features and interventions into the most ambitious product of the field: a mechanistic story of a behavior from input to output, of the kind the biology paper tells.

| question | move | method | chapter |
|---|---|---|---|
| What is the model's running guess? | observe | logit lens | @sec-logit-lens |
| What concepts is it using? | observe | sparse autoencoders | @sec-sparse-autoencoders |
| Which internal state causes the output? | intervene | activation patching | @sec-activation-patching |
| How do the parts compose into an algorithm? | intervene | circuits | @sec-circuits |

The order is deliberate, and it is an old rule of fieldwork: you describe a culture before you try to change it. Observation first, intervention after.

::: {.callout-tip}
You can do the observational fieldwork today, with no code and no setup. [Neuronpedia](https://www.neuronpedia.org) hosts browsable dashboards for thousands of SAE features in open models — pick one and try to infer what it means from the contexts where it fires. The [NDIF Workbench](https://workbench.ndif.us) (from David Bau's lab at Northeastern) runs the logit lens live on a real model in your browser; a free one-click account gets you in. Type a fill-in-the-blank whose answer you know and watch where in the model's depth the answer locks in.
:::

<!-- DRAFT NOTE (Emre): the SICSS hands-on ran "The Galata Tower is in the city of ___" on the Workbench — the model holds "Galata" through the middle layers and only resolves "Istanbul" at the top: you watch a fact being computed, not looked up. The participants' screenshots became "the room's first field notes." That story, told in your voice, belongs here or in the logit-lens chapter. -->

## Whose science is this?

Now return to the question I asked you to hold: does AI need social science, or does social science need AI? Here is my answer, after what you have seen.

Look at what reading a model actually *is*, method by method. Interpreting an SAE feature means looking at the contexts where it fires and inferring the category — that is qualitative coding, exactly, with an n of a few dozen text snippets and a label you commit to. The auto-generated label is one coder; you, disagreeing with it in the dashboard, are the second coder; the disagreement rate is inter-rater reliability. Watching the logit lens form an answer across layers is process-tracing a decision. And the field's hard problems are our hard problems, one for one. Does this feature mean what its label says? That is construct validity. Does the reading replicate on new prompts? Reliability. Are we measuring the model, or an artifact of our instrument? Measurement error — and it is not hypothetical: [Li et al. (2025)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.16004) show that the concept readings SAEs produce are not robust — small, targeted changes to the input text can manipulate which features fire — and the behavioral side has the same disease: LLM "personality" scores shift substantially under prompt paraphrase and answer-option reordering ([Gupta et al. 2024](https://aclanthology.org/2024.blackboxnlp-1.20/)), and LLM "political compass" results depend heavily on forcing the model through a multiple-choice format it would not otherwise follow ([Röttger et al. 2024](https://aclanthology.org/2024.acl-long.816/)). A century of methods training, suddenly load-bearing in someone else's field.

And here is the part I find genuinely satisfying: social scientists were doing this *before* the machine-learning world arrived at it. In 2019, in the *American Sociological Review*, [Kozlowski, Taddy and Evans](https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/0003122419877135) measured social class and gender as directions in word-vector space and called it "the geometry of culture" — the same concepts-as-directions move that superposition research and Piantadosi's argument converge on, published in a sociology journal before the field had a name for features. And the intervention tradition has been claimed from inside the discipline too: [Ji Ma (2026)](https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/00811750261421220), in *Sociological Methodology*, extracts the direction along which a model's internal state varies between "male" and "female" personas and injects perturbations along it to test, causally, how gender is wired into the model's decisions in a classic behavioral game. That is activation-level experimentation, peer-reviewed as sociology.

So my answer: the dependency runs one way. Social science can proceed without AI — it did for a century. But a trustworthy science of AI cannot proceed without the people whose entire training is reading minds they did not build and knowing whether the reading is valid. AI does not need us to make bigger models. It needs us to say what a measurement means.

<!-- DRAFT NOTE (Emre): this is the Phase 5 tie-up of the talk — your personal answer, delivered after the room's re-vote. Consider restating it here in first person with whatever the actual vote shift was. -->

## In research

For your own work, interpretability enters at three levels, in increasing order of ambition.

The first is **auditing your instrument**. If the populism classifier from the opening paragraph is a black box, your measurement section is an act of faith. The observational tools change that in kind, not just in degree: you can ask which internal features are active when the model labels a speech populist, and check whether they are features *about* anti-elite framing or features about exclamation marks and informal register. This is the same validation logic you would apply to a human coder — ask them why — finally applicable to the machine coder you are actually using. It will not be exhaustive, and the warnings above apply, but "we probed the basis of the classifications" is a defensible sentence in a methods section in a way that "the model agreed with our coders" alone is not.

The second is **features as measurement instruments**. Kozlowski and colleagues projected words onto a class axis built from static embeddings; the modern versions of that move project *any text* onto directions found inside a full language model — directions for concepts as narrow as a particular legal institution or as loaded as moralized language. Where a dictionary method gives you word counts and an embedding gives you one similarity score, a feature dashboard gives you a concept with receipts: every context in the corpus where it fires, ready to be read, coded, and challenged. For survey open-ends, interview transcripts, party manifestos, news coverage — any corpus where you currently trade validity for scale — this is a third instrument class, sitting between the dictionary and the black box.

The third is **the model as the object of study itself**. A growing research program treats LLMs as simulated respondents and agents in social experiments; whatever you think of that program, its validity question is internal, not behavioral — *what is the machinery behind the simulated answer?* — and Ma's paper shows what answering it looks like inside a sociology journal. The same applies to the politics of the models themselves: claims that a model "leans left" or "encodes stereotypes" are measurement claims about an opaque instrument, and both the behavioral critique (Röttger et al.) and the internal evidence (features, directions, circuits) are now part of what a rigorous version of that claim requires. If your research touches how these systems are audited, governed, or deployed in institutions, the internal evidence is where those arguments are heading.

One habit to carry across this whole part of the book, the same one @sec-embeddings taught for cosine scores: every reading of a model's insides is **a reading, not a proof** — a plausible description built on selected prompts, smaller models, and labels that do not always replicate. That is not a reason to dismiss the field. It is a description of a young science, and an accurate description of plenty of ours. We spent a century learning to study people who do not fully know their own minds. Now there is a new kind of mind — and it is waiting for its anthropologists.

## Further reading

- [Lindsey et al. (2025), *On the Biology of a Large Language Model*, Transformer Circuits](https://transformer-circuits.pub/2025/attribution-graphs/biology.html) — the paper behind the video; skim the case studies even if the methods are ahead of you. The [film version](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Bj9BD2D3DzA) is the best short introduction to the field that exists.
- [Amodei (2025), *The Urgency of Interpretability*](https://www.darioamodei.com/post/the-urgency-of-interpretability) — why the builders themselves think reading these models is urgent; the source for "grown, not built."
- [Rahwan et al. (2019), "Machine behaviour," *Nature*](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41586-019-1138-y) — the founding manifesto for studying machines with behavioral science, written before LLMs.
- [Fodor & Pylyshyn (1988), "Connectionism and cognitive architecture," *Cognition*](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/0010027788900315) and [Piantadosi et al. (2024), "Why concepts are (probably) vectors," *Trends in Cognitive Sciences*](https://www.cell.com/trends/cognitive-sciences/fulltext/S1364-6613(24)00171-2) — the two poles of the argument this part of the book turns into an experiment.
- [Ferrando et al. (2024), *A Primer on the Inner Workings of Transformer-based Language Models*](https://arxiv.org/abs/2405.00208) — the technical survey to keep at your elbow through the next four chapters.
- [Kozlowski, Taddy & Evans (2019), "The Geometry of Culture," *American Sociological Review*](https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/0003122419877135) and [Ma (2026), "Computational Basis of Large Language Models' Decision Making in Social Simulation," *Sociological Methodology*](https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/00811750261421220) — proof, six years apart, that both the observational and the interventionist traditions belong to social science.
- Tools to bookmark now: [Neuronpedia](https://www.neuronpedia.org) for feature dashboards, and the [NDIF Workbench](https://workbench.ndif.us) for the logit lens in a browser.
